coredata.dc:title: Shape-dependant photocatalytic and antimicrobial activity of ZnO nanostructures when conjugated to graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Tshangana, Charmaine
Chabalala, Mandla
Muleja, Adolph
Nxumalo, Edward
Mamba, Bhekie
coredata.dc:description: To reduce the bandgap and e−/h+ pairs recombination, zinc oxide nanoflakes (ZnO-NFs) and zinc oxide nanorods (ZnO-NRs) were covalently linked to graphene oxide quantum dots (GQDs). Photocatalytic and antimicrobial activity of ZnO nanohybrids (ZnO-NFs@GQDs and ZnO-NRs@GQDs) were investigated. The formation of the distinct ZnO nanoflakes and ZnO nanorods shaped nanoparticles were evidenced by SEM. The bandgap decreased from 2.98 to 2.61 eV and 3.00–2.79 eV for ZnO-NFs@GQDs and ZnO-NRs@GQDs, respectively. Photoluminescence (PL) data showed a similar trend where a larger decrease was obtained for ZnO-NFs@GQDs. Photolysis ruled out adsorption mechanism for the removal of dye. Optical data and PL measurements supported photocatalytic findings that ZnO-NFs@GQDs performed better (80 %) than ZnO-NRs@GQDs (18 %). ZnO-NRs@GQDs however, showed greater bacterial inhibition when tested against E. coli, P. aeruginosa, B. cereus and S. aureus. The antimicrobial efficiency followed the order: ZnO-NFs < ZnO-NFs@GQDs < ZnO-NRs < ZnO-NRs@GQDs with greater efficiency being against S. aureus. Liquid photocatalysts zinc nanoparticles can thus be used to remove dyes and inhibit bacterial growth in aqueous solution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zinc oxide nanorods
Zinc oxide nanoflakes
Photocatalysis
Antimicrobial activity
Graphene quantum dots

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issfirst issn issnnorm issuelist itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid misctext orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Amino functionalization of ZnO nanoflakes and ZnO nanorods
Covalently linking amino functionalized ZnO nanoflakes or ZnO nanorods to GQDs
Antimicrobial activity of the ZnO-NFs@GQDs and ZnO-NRs@GQDs nanohybrids

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the covalent linkage of the graphene oxide quantum dots (GQDs) to (A) ZnO-NFs and (B) ZnO-NRs.
SEM images of (A) bare ZnO-NFs and (B) ZnO-NFs@GQDs nanohybrid and their corresponding size distribution histograms.
SEM images of (a) bare ZnO-NRs and (b) ZnO-NRs@GQDs nanohybrid (insert) x 5000 magnification of ZnO-NRs@GQDs nanohybrid and corresponding size distribution histograms.
(A) FT-IR spectra of (i) ZnO-NRs@GQDs (ii) ZnO-NRs (iii) ZnO-NFs@GQDs (iv) ZnO-NFs and (B) Raman spectra of (i) GQDs and (C) Raman spectra of (i) ZnO-NFs and (ii) ZnO-NRs.
XRD patterns of the synthesized (A) (i) ZnO-NFs and (ii) ZnO-NRs and (B) (i) GQDs and (C) (i) ZnO-NFs@GQDs and (ii) ZnO-NRs@GQDs.
UV–vis spectra showing (A) ground-state absorption of (i) ZnO-NRs and (ii) ZnO-NFs (B) (i) emission and (ii) absorption of GQDs and (C) Tauc’s plot of the prepared (i) ZnO-NFs and (ii) ZnO-NRs.

(A) Ground-state absorption spectra of (i) ZnO-NFs@GQDs and (ii) ZnO-NRs@GQDs nanohybrids, (B) Photolumiscence spectra of ZnO-NRs and ZnO-NRs@GQDs nanohybrid and (C) Photolumiscence spectra of ZnO-NFs and ZnO-NFs@GQDs nanohybrids. Excitation wavelength 325 nm.

Spectral changes observed during photodegradation of BB dye with (A) ZnO-NFs@GQDs and (i) Image of BB dye solution before and after photodegradation experiment with ZnO-NFs@GQDS nanohybrid under white LED strips. (B) ZnO-NRs@GQDs and (i) Image of BB dye solution before and after photodegradation experiment with ZnO-NRs@GQDS nanohybrid under white LED strips. Time interval =30 min.

(A) Spectral changes observed during photodegradation of BB dye in the absence of photocatalysts. Time interval =30 min, (B) Photocatalytic degradation of BB dye by (i) ZnO-NRs (ii) ZnO-NRs@GQDs (iii) ZnO-NFs and (iv) ZnO-NFs@GQDs under white LED strips. Time interval =30 min.
Calculated Colony-forming unit E.coli, P. aeruginosa, B.cereus and S.aureus when tested against ZnO-NRs, ZnO-NRs@GQDs, ZnO-NFs and ZnO-NFs@GQDs.
Inhibitory effect of ZnO-NRs, ZnO-NFs, ZnO-NRs@GQDs and ZnO-NFs@GQDs on Mueller Hinton agar plates tested against E.coli, P. aeruginosa B. cereus and S.aureus.
Band gaps of the ZnO nanoparticles calculated using Eq. 2 and extrapolated from Tauc’s plot.
Rate constant (kobs), and half-life (t 1/2) and r2 values of various initial concentrations of BB dye.

head.ce:title: Shape-dependant photocatalytic and antimicrobial activity of ZnO nanostructures when conjugated to graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology and Water Sustainability Research Unit. College of Science, Engineering and Technology, University of South Africa, Florida, 1709, Johannesburg, South Africa

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanotechnology and Water Sustainability Research Unit
College of Science, Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanotechnology and Water Sustainability Research Unit. College of Science, Engineering and Technology, University of South Africa, Florida, 1709, Johannesburg, South Africa.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To reduce the bandgap and e−/h+ pairs recombination, zinc oxide nanoflakes (ZnO-NFs) and zinc oxide nanorods (ZnO-NRs) were covalently linked to graphene oxide quantum dots (GQDs). Photocatalytic and antimicrobial activity of ZnO nanohybrids (ZnO-NFs@GQDs and ZnO-NRs@GQDs) were investigated. The formation of the distinct ZnO nanoflakes and ZnO nanorods shaped nanoparticles were evidenced by SEM. The bandgap decreased from 2.98 to 2.61 eV and 3.00–2.79 eV for ZnO-NFs@GQDs and ZnO-NRs@GQDs, respectively. Photoluminescence (PL) data showed a similar trend where a larger decrease was obtained for ZnO-NFs@GQDs. Photolysis ruled out adsorption mechanism for the removal of dye. Optical data and PL measurements supported photocatalytic findings that ZnO-NFs@GQDs performed better (80 %) than ZnO-NRs@GQDs (18 %). ZnO-NRs@GQDs however, showed greater bacterial inhibition when tested against E. coli, P. aeruginosa, B. cereus and S. aureus. The antimicrobial efficiency followed the order: ZnO-NFs < ZnO-NFs@GQDs < ZnO-NRs < ZnO-NRs@GQDs with greater efficiency being against S. aureus. Liquid photocatalysts zinc nanoparticles can thus be used to remove dyes and inhibit bacterial growth in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:para: Wastewater discharged from textile factories contain a mixture of dyes and chemicals which are a major environmental pollution threat [1,2]. A lot of energy and research has focussed on developing novel nanohybrids to remove these undesirable species in industrial wastewater and render it potable. Advanced oxidative processes (AOPs) have been at the forefront of wastewater treatment due to its cost-effectiveness, rapid rate of decomposition and complete degradation of organic matter and by-products [3,4]. The most widely applied AOP is photocatalysis, wherein the photocatalyst can inactivate microorganisms and degrade organic materials by producing reactive oxygen species (ROS) upon absorption of sunlight [5]. Typically, semiconducting metal oxides are preferred photocatalyts because they generate the much-needed electron-hole pairs for redox reactions [6,7].

ZnO has been identified as the most preferred photocatalyst due to its high photocatalytic efficiency and chemical activities. In addition, it was found to be more biocompatible than TiO2 [8]. The attractiveness of ZnO is as a result of its unique properties, such as a wide band gap energy of 3.37 eV, large excitation binding energy (60 meV), high electron mobility, transparency in the visible range and a low-cost production [9,10]. The morphology of the ZnO nanoparticles (NPs) depends largely on the synthetic procedure. During the synthesis process various parameters such as solvents, precursor types, temperature and pH can be varied and tailored to yield the desired shape and size [11]. Numerous shapes and sizes of ZnO NPs have been reported [12–14] and it has been found that the differently shaped nanoparticles exhibit unique optical, structural, physico-chemical and antimicrobial properties therefore one would expect them to perform differently.

In this work, we investigate the shape effect of the ZnO NPs on the photocatalytic and antimicrobial properties when ZnO nanoflakes (NFs) or ZnO nanorods (NRs) are covalently linked to graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to form either ZnO-NFs@GQDs or ZnO-NRs@GQDs nanohybrids. The choice to link GQDs to ZnO-NFs or ZnO-NRs originated from the need to overcome the two major limitations of ZnO as a photocatalyst which include; the rapid rate of electron-hole pair recombination and its inability to harvest a large part of the sunlight as a result of their large band-gaps. Covalently linking GQDs to ZnO NPs seeks to improve the performance of ZnO NFs or ZnO-NRs by: (a) delaying the rate of recombination of electron-hole pairs as the GQDs are able to act as barriers allowing them to remain active a little longer and (b) enabling better harnessing of the sunlight because of the π–π* transition of GQDs in the visible region. Reports have therefore documented the use of GQDs in improving the photocatalytic performance of ZnO [15–18]. However, reports on the comparative antimicrobial efficiency of ZnO-NRs and ZnO-NFs when covalently linked to GQDs on Escherichia coli (E.coli), Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) and Bacillus cereus (B. cereus) are not readily available in the literature. In addition to improving the photocatalytic properties of ZnO NPs, our hypothesis is also to take advantage of GQDs’ ability to significantly inactivate microorganisms and envisage that covalently linking the GQDs to ZnO-NFs and ZnO-NRs will result in nanohybrids with enhanced antimicrobial properties. The aim of this work was to: (a) investigate to what extent the shape (nanoflakes or nanorods) of the ZnO plays in the photocatalytic properties of its nanohybrid with GQDs when Brilliant black (BB) was used as a model dye and (b) to establish which of the two morphologies (nanoflakes or nanorods) exhibit better antimicrobial activity and try understand the inhibition mechanism based on the structure of the ZnO NPs and (c) to study whether there is a direct correlation between the photocatalytic activity and the antimicrobial activity hence allowing the nanohybrids to be applied as “dual nanohybrids”.

Zinc acetate dehydrate (Zn(CH3COO)2), ammonium hydroxide (30 % NH3 in H2O), 3-aminopropyl-triethoxysilane (APTES), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), citric acid and silica gel for chromatography, dimethylformamide (DMF), toluene, ethanol (C2H6) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) and Brilliant black (BB) dye were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, South Africa. Meuller-Hinton (MH) agar and broth and Nutrient Agar (NA) were also purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, South Africa. Phosphate-buffered solutions of pH 7.4 and 9 were prepared using appropriate amounts of disodium phosphate (Na2HPO4), potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4) and chloride salts, dissolved in ultra-pure water. Sodium hydroxide solutions (pH 9) were prepared by using accurately weighed amounts of sodium hydroxide pellets dissolved in ultra-pure water from Milli-Q-ultra pure water system (Millipore Corp., Bedford, MA, USA). All solvents and reagents used were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Amino functionalization of ZnO nanoflakes and ZnO nanorods
ce:section.ce:para: The nanoparticles and quantum dots were synthesized as per methods previously reported in literature for ZnO-NFs [19], ZnO-NRs [20] and GQDs [21]. They were then functionalized following a published method [22] with slight modifications which is briefly described. In this work, samples of ZnO-NFs or ZnO-NRs (10 g) were first washed with anhydrous ethanol followed by toluene. The washed ZnO-NFs or ZnO-NRs were then added to 12 mL of DMF and 8 mL of toluene and the mixture was stirred. The mixture was suspended in an ultrasonic bath for 10 min after which (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES 1 mL) was added dropwise using a syringe needle. The resultant mixture was stirred for 24 h at room temperature under argon gas flow. The prepared amino functionalized ZnO-NFs or ZnO-NRs were centrifuged and washed four times with copious amounts of ethanol. The products were dried at 50 °C for 60 min and later grinded and kept in the oven for 2 h at 100 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Covalently linking amino functionalized ZnO nanoflakes or ZnO nanorods to GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The resultant ZnO NPs were covalently linked to GQDs as shown in Scheme 1 . The method of linking the amino functionalized ZnO-NFs or ZnO-NRs to GQDs employs EDC/NHS coupling chemistry as described by Tshangana et al. [23] where they linked GSH-CdSe@ZnS quantum dots to a metallated phthalocyanine. In this procedure, the carboxyl groups of GQDs were activated by dissolving GQDs (10 mg) in 10 mL of dilute NaOH, followed by the addition of EDC (2 mL, 1.2 mM). The mixture was stirred for 48 h at room temperature under argon gas flow. The amino functionalized ZnO-NFs or ZnO-NRs (5 mg) were suspended in 2 mL of 1 mM NHS and subsequently added to the activated GQDs. The solution was stirred further for 12 h at room temperature under argon gas flow to allow the conjugation of the GQDs to the ZnO-NFs or ZnO-NRs. The product was precipitated by the addition of ethanol and washed with copious amounts of ethanol. Size exclusion chromatography (using an aqueous solution at pH 9 as the eluting solvent) was used to remove the GQDs that had not adhered to the ZnO-NFs or ZnO-NRs. The conjugates of the amino functionalized ZnO-NRs or ZnO-NFs linked to GQD are represented as ZnO-NRs@GQDs and ZnO-NFs@GQDs nanohybrids.

Ground state electronic absorption spectra was recorded using UV–vis-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu 1800). Infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker Alpha IR (100 FT-IR). Confocal Raman spectrometer equipped with 514 nm laser was used to collect spectral data. The morphology of the ZnO NPs samples was examined using an FEI QUANTA 200 F Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM) High-resolution Scanning Electron Microscope HR-(SEM). Prior to the SEM measurements, the sample was coated with gold using a sputter coater (Balzers Union SCD 030). Photolumiscence spectra were analysed using Horiba Fluorolog 3 FL3, xenon lamp. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to obtain XRD patterns and determine size of NPs. From XRD data, the average crystallite size of the NPs were determined using the Debye-Scherrer equation (Eq. 1) [24].
(1)
d =
0.9
λ
B c o s θ
where d is the mean diameter of a quantum dot in nanometers (nm), λ the wavelength of the X-ray source (1.5405 Å), β the full width at half maximum of the diffraction peak and θ, the angular position of the peak.

The band gaps were calculated using (Eq. 2)
(2)
B a n d
g a p
e V
=
h
c
λ
where Eg is the band gap in eV, h is planks constant (6.626 × 10−34 Js-1), c is the speed of light (3.00 × 108 ms-1) and λ is the wavelength in nm.

The photolytic and photocatalytic experiments were carried out with white Light Emitting Diodes (LED) strips. The LED lights (20 mW) were positioned around the circumference in a customized stainless-steel rectangular reactor which consisted of a magnetic stirrer and beaker. The experimental setup was shielded from environmental light using an aluminium foil cover. The extent of degradation of brilliant black (BB) dye by the synthesized ZnO-NRs@GQDs and ZnO-NFs@GQDs nanohybrids was studied by monitoring the decolorization of the BB solution. The initial concentration of the organic pollutant model (BB dyes) was 10 ppm and 1 mL of ZnO-NRs@GQDs or ZnO-NFs@GQDs was added to the BB dye solution (50 mL). The solution was kept in the dark for 30 min to allow adsorption and desorption equilibrium. The absorbance of the solution was measured every 30 min (for a total of 180 min). All the photocatalytic experiments were performed three times to ensure reproducibility of the experiments, and the average values are reported. The photolytic experiments were conducted in a similar manner to those of the photocatalytic experiments under the white light LED strips without adding homogeneous catalysts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial activity of the ZnO-NFs@GQDs and ZnO-NRs@GQDs nanohybrids

ce:section.ce:para: The effects of the ZnO-NFs@GQDs and ZnO-NRs@GQDs on Escherichia coli (E. coli), Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) and Bacillus cereus (B. cereus) were studied using the disc diffusion method as described by Vijayakumar et al. [25]. The bacterial strains were grown on Mueller-Hinton agar, prepared as per manufacturer’s specifications. One colony was inoculated into fresh Mueller-Hinton broth and the four bacterial strains were grown for 24 h at 37 °C with mild shaking to mid-log phase (OD600 = 0.6). The antimicrobial efficiency of the ZnO-NRs, ZnO-NFs, ZnO-NRs@GQDs or ZnO-NFs@GQDs (4 mg/mL) were assessed against bacterial suspensions (grown in bacterial lawn) with turbidity equivalent to 0.5 MacFarland standard on Mueller-Hinton agar plates. Blank discs were placed on the surface of the inoculated plate to which 20 μL of sample (ZnO-NRs, ZnO-NFs, ZnO-NRs@GQDs or ZnO-NFs@GQDs) was added and the plates were incubated at 37 °C for a period of 24 h. The zones where bacterial growth was not observed was recorded as inhibition zones (distance from disc circumference in mm) which was measured for each disc. The experiments were repeated three times to ensure repeatability and the average values are reported.

To get an estimate of the number of viable bacteria, colony-forming unit (CFU) values were calculated using (Eq. 3 )
(3)
C F U / m L =
T o t a l
c o l o n i e s
x
d i l u t i o n
f a c t o r
V o l u m e
p l a t e d

From the broth culture, 1 mL of each bacterial strain was diluted 50 times with sterile deionized water and 0.1 mL of the diluted solution was plated on a nutrient agar (NA) plate. The plates were then incubated for 24 h at 37 °C to allow bacterial growth and the colonies counted.

High-resolution scanning electron microscopy (HR-SEM) images (Figs. 1 and 2
) confirmed that the synthesized ZnO nanoparticles were indeed shaped differently and enabled us to study the effect of shape on the photocatalytic and antimicrobial properties. Aggregated spherical ZnO-NFs are seen in Fig. 1 (A). The average sizes were estimated by plotting histograms using Image J software and were determined to be 23.5 nm. Upon conjugating GQDs to the ZnO-NFs (Fig. 1 (B)), there is no noticeable changes in the morphology however there is an increase in size of the ZnO-NFs@GQDs nanohybrid to 68.57 nm which confirms that GQDs were successfully linked to the ZnO-NFs.

Fig. 2 (A) demonstrated distinct rod shaped ZnO-NRs with an average diameter of 53.75 nm where the incorporation of GQDs resulted in the nanohybrids forming clusters (Fig. 2 (B)). The sizes of the individual ZnO-NRs@GQDs nanohybrids could not be estimated, hence the size of the bulky nanohybrid clusters were measured and was estimated to be 423.36 nm. Higher magnification (insert on Fig. 2 (B)) showed that while the nanohybrid aggregated and formed clusters, they still maintained their rod-like structure, making it effective for our shape-dependant investigations. This also shows that the incorporation of GQDs to the nanohybrids does not alter the original shape of the ZnO-NRs.

The covalent link between ZnO-NFs or ZnO-NRs with the GQDs was confirmed using FTIR spectroscopy as shown in (Fig. 3 (A). Upon conjugating ZnO-NFs or ZnO-NRs with the GQDs (Fig. 3 (A) (i and iii)) we note the emergence of peaks assigned to the amide group (-NHCO-) such as CO at 1657 cm −1, –NH- bend at 1566 cm−1 and the –NH stretch around ∼ 3000 cm−1 confirming the successful linking of the ZnO-NFs or ZnO-NRs to the GQDs. Both ZnO-NFs and ZnO-NRs (Fig. 3 (A) (ii and iv) show the characteristic peaks of the Si-O-Si at 1034 cm-1 and for ZnO at 421 cm-1 with a Zn-O stretching vibrational peak at 574 cm−1 indicating that the nanoparticles have the wurtzite crystal structure [26]. ZnO-NRs have two additional peaks at 1392 and 1579 cm−1 attributed to symmetric and asymmetric CO double bond vibrations due to the ionisation of carbonyl groups [27,28]. The OH peak at 3450 cm−1 observed for both ZnO-NFs and ZnO-NRs samples is attributed to atmospheric moisture.

Fig. 3 (B) shows characteristic Raman peaks of the GQDs two prominent peaks around ∼1350 and 1600 cm−1 corresponding to the D and G bands, which are indicative of the successful synthesis of the GQDs. For the ZnO-NFs and ZnO-NRs peaks labelled a, b, c, d and e, which correspond to E2 (low) – 103 cm−1, 2E2 – 332 cm−1, A1 (TO) – 386 cm−1, E2 (high) – 444 cm−1 and A1 (LO) – 578 cm−1 overlapped with E1 (LO) – 592 cm−1, respectively are seen in Fig. 3 (C). Peak b (2E2) is referred to as the 2nd order Raman spectrum from the zone bound ZnO phonons. A typical Raman spectra for ZnO has five potential Raman modes: A1 (TO), A1 (LO), 2B1, E1 (low) and E2 (high) [29]. 2B1 is often referred to as the silent mode as a result of it not being Raman active, however, not all active modes can always be observed in the spectrum. When the incident light is normal to the surface of ZnO (incident and scattered polarization parallel) only A1 (LO) and E2 are observed. In the case of perpendicular polarization, only E2 is observed. However, it is highly possible to observe all the modes at once [[29], [30] [31],].

XRD analysis was carried out to evaluate the crystalline structure of the ZnO NPs and GQDs ( Fig. 4 (A and B). The XRD peaks for the prepared ZnO nanoparticles match the previously reported peaks [32], the phase of the ZnO NPs is hexagonal wurtzite with lattice constants of a = 3.26 Å and c = 5.21 Å. The sharp diffraction peaks and the absence of secondary phase indicates the purity of the ZnO NPs. In comparison, the XRD pattern of the GQDs showed a broad peak at 2θ = 24 which corresponds with XRD data reported for synthesized GQDs [33]. The average crystallite sizes were determined using the Debye-Scherrer equation and were found to be 2.1 nm for the GQDs and 5.7 nm and 6.3 nm for ZnO-NRs and ZnO-NFs, respectively.

Upon conjugating GQDs to the ZnO nanostructures, the average crystallite sizes were determined as16.6 nm and 17.4 nm for the ZnO-NRs@GQDs and ZnO-NFs@GQDs conjugates, respectively. The XRD patterns of the ZnO-NRs@GQDs and ZnO-NFs@GQDs conjugates (Fig. 4 (C)), maintained the characteristic ZnO peaks as seen in Fig. 4 (A), with a slight shift of the main ZnO peak between 35° and 38° to a lower degree. This is a result of the addition of GQDs, which result in the increase of lattice constants of ZnO. A new diffraction peak at 26.35° attributed to the (0 0 2) peak of graphite emerges, indicating that the GQDs were successfully conjugated to the ZnO nanostructures and this correlates well with what has been reported by Xu et al. for ZnO - graphene composites [34].

The optical properties of the nanomaterials were studied using spectrophotometry as shown in Fig. 5 . The reflectance of both the ZnO-NRs and ZnO-NFs is noted at approximately 400 nm (Fig. 5 (A), which corresponds to previously reported reflectance of ZnO nanoparticles [35]. For the GQDs ( Fig. 5 (B)), a well-defined optical absorption in the UV region with a tail extended in the visible region at 351 nm is observed which is attributed to a π–π* transition of aromatic CC bonds [36]. The GQDs display a narrow well-defined emission with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) at 480 nm when excited at 390 nm. The band gaps of the ZnO-NRs, ZnO-NFs were calculated using Eq. 2. Furthermore, Tauc’s Plot (Fig. 5 (C)) was used to extrapolate the band gaps by plotting (αhv)1/2 vs hv where α is the relative reflectance and hv = hc/λ. The band gaps obtained for the ZnO-NRs and ZnO-NFs show a negligible difference of approximately 0.2 eV. The calculated band gaps of the ZnO-NRs and ZnO-NFs are summarized in Table 1 .

Both ZnO-NFs and ZnO-NRs nanoparticles were expected to have the reported band gaps of pristine ZnO (∼3.4 eV). However, in this study we obtained ∼3.0 eV for both ZnO-NFs (2.98 eV) and ZnO-NRs (3.00 eV). The reduced bang gap obtained here is due to the O atoms that act as impurities or defects. These impurities are the same as those that are introduced by dopants such as metals or dye sensitizers in the case of doped-ZnO with low band gap. The principle of band gap reduction is based on impurities found on the crystal structure of semiconductors [31].

To further confirm the successful linking of the ZnO-NFs or ZnO-NRs to GQDs UV absorption was run (Fig. 6 (A)). Typical absorption spectra of ZnO NPs and GQDs ( Fig. 5 A and B) do not have two peaks, however in the nanohybrids two characteristic peaks belonging to the ZnO and GQDs were observed. There is a significant blue shift in the reflectance of the nanohybrids which is attributed to domination of quantum confinement effect after incorporating GQDs. The presence of GQDs is marked by the emergence of the peak at 396 nm in the ZnO-NRs@GQDs conjugate, while the peak attributed to the ZnO is seen at 419 nm. For the ZnO-NRs@GQDs, the peak associated with GQDs now observed at 403 nm while for the ZnO peak it is still at 419 nm. Bandgap energies of the nanohybrids were estimated from Eq. 2. In this work the value for n was set at ½, due to ZnO showing a direct transition between its valence and conduction band [37]. The calculated bandgaps of the nanohybrids decreased from 2.98 eV to 2.61 eV and from 3.00 eV to 2.79 eV for the ZnO-NFs@GQDs and ZnO-NRs@GQDs respectively. The decrease in bandgap allows the nanohybrids to harvest solar light, thus covalently linking GQDs to ZnO-NPs increases the photocatalytic potential, thus making them ideal photocatalysts. ZnO-NFs@GQDs nanohybrid had a larger decrease in bandgap after linking GQDs making it a better candidate as a photocatalyst than the ZnO-NFs@GQDs nanohybrid.

The rate of photodegradation is influenced heavily by the recombination of electron-hole pairs which can be understood by analysing the photolumiscence (PL) spectra. The PL spectra of ZnO-NRs and ZnO-NFs alone were compared to the PL spectra of the nanohybrids (ZnO-NRs@GQDs and ZnO-NFs@GQDs). Equivalent concentrations (4 mg/mL) of all four samples (ZnO-NRs, ZnO-NFs, ZnO-NRs@GQDs and ZnO-NFs@GQDs) were dissolved in Millipore water and excited at 325 nm. An intense sharp narrow peak (Fig. 6 (B and C)) was observed at 397 nm for bare ZnO-NRs and ZnO-NFs and weak broad peaks for both ZnO-NRs and ZnO-NFs in the region ∼ 490 nm. The intense peaks in the UV region are as a result of the recombination of free excitons and the broader peaks are attributed to structural defects and singly ionized vacancies. This is typical when recombination of a photogenerated hole has a singly ionized charged state [38,39]. Upon incorporating GQDs (Fig. 6 B and C), we observed weaker PL in both samples, with the ZnO-NFs@GQDs nanohybrid showing the most decrease and a complete disappearance of the broad band in the green region. For the ZnO-NRs@GQDs the decrease which was not significant was accompanied by a peak broadening and shift to 411 nm the shift is attributed to the addition of GQDs which led to increase in quantum confinement.

The significant decrease in PL after incorporating GQDs signals a decrease in recombination rate of electron hole pairs. Akhavan et al. [40] showed that the presence of GQDs on the ZnO-NPs can capture the photogenerated electrons thus preventing the recombination with their hole counterparts. Electron-hole pairs that are not trapped usually recombine and as a result these electrons or holes do not participate in photocatalytic reactions. These findings correspond to the results obtained for calculation of the bandgaps and the UV data which all are in favour of the ZnO-NFs@GQDs nanohybrid as the better photocatalyst. In relation to the morphologies of the nanohybrids, the better photocatalytic performance is attributed to the larger surface area of the ZnO-NFs@GQDs nanohybrid which allows more absorption of the solar energy as evidenced by the greater absorption in the visible region and the significant reduction in PL compared to the ZnO-NRs@GQDs nanohybrid. The effective surface area (adsorbed amount of target molecules) is a great indicator of photocatalytic potential [41]. Surface area and surface defects play an important role in the photocatalytic activity of metal-oxide nanostructures, as the contaminant molecules need to be adsorbed onto the photocatalytic surface for the redox reactions to occur. The higher the effective surface area, the higher will be the adsorption of target molecules leading to better photocatalytic activity. Our findings contradict that of Manriquez et al. [42] who found the photoactivity of the ZnO nanorods to be slightly superior to that of the nanoparticles. The ZnO nano flowers were found to be more efficient than ZnO nanorods for the degradation of 4-chlorophenol under UV light irradiation.

The extent of BB dye degradation was estimated from absorbance spectra; intact BB dye shows strong absorbance at 573 nm. Loss of intensity and shift in the BB peak position was considered as degradation. In Fig. 7 (A) and (B), spectral changes of BB dye upon illumination in the presence of the nanohybrids are shown. In Fig. 7 (A) a significant decrease in absorbance of the BB dye peak in the presence of ZnO-NFs@GQDs was observed. The maximum absorption peak of the BB dye at 573 nm decreased significantly with time, a noticeable red-shifting of the BB peak was observed with increase in time. The reduction in absorbance in the visible region indicated the decolourization through deprotonation (cleavage of the N––N bond), desulfonation and desulfonation (ring opening) [43,44]. The decrease in peak absorbance is associated with the decolourization of the BB dye solution Fig. 7 insert. When ZnO-NRs@GQDs nanohybrid was used as a photocatalyst (Fig. 7 (B)) there was a gradual decrease of BB peak over time, not as significant as that of the ZnO-NFs@GQDs, similarly the colour change was not as significant (Fig. 7 (B) insert).

To determine whether the degradation was as a result of the nanohybrids or light, experiments were carried out in the absence of the nanohybrids and there was very little decrease on the BB peak on the photolysis in the absence of the photocatalysts (Fig. 8
(A)), proving that the nanohybrids are responsible for the degradation of the BB dye. In addition, further photodegradation experiments using ZnO-NRs and ZnO-NFs without the GQDs were also performed, however the changes on the BB peak were not as significant. The role of GQDs in the nanohybrid is to increase the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and as a result increase the photocatalytic efficiency. It is worth noting that the ZnO-NFs (with and without GQDs) performed better.

Kinetic data as listed in Table 2 was obtained by plotting the varying concentrations of the BB dye versus the irradiation time. The plots obtained were linear when plotting I n =
C 0
C
versus time suggesting a pseudo-first order kinetics reaction. We noted a decrease in the reactions rates (kobs ) rate in the order ZnO-NRs < ZnO-NRs@GQDs < ZnO-NFs < ZnO-NFs@GQDs as the concentration of the BB dye was increased. The decrease was expected as an increase in BB dye concentration results in photons entering the solution being intercepted, thus reducing photodegradation rates of BB dye at higher concentrations [45]. At lower concentrations the reverse is true with a higher number of protons being absorbed, in addition a trend was noted an increase in reaction rate also yielded shorter half-lives.

ZnO-NFs@GQDs had the largest K obs than all photocatalysts, this could be due to improved ROS generation and as a result improved photocatalytic efficiency. ZnO-NRs without GQDs showed the least photocatalytic performance. The half-lives were also calculated, a half-life indicates the time it would takes for half of the BB dye to be degraded, and the half-lives the nanohybrids were shorter and these were accompanied by quicker reaction rates. From literature [46], it is found that pseudo-ﬁrst-order Langmuir–Hinshelwood kinetics is the best model to describe the influence of initial dye concentration on the photocatalytic transformation and governs the degradation of organic dyes Eq. 4
(4) C = C 0e −kt or − Ln(Ct/C0) = kt
where C0 is the initial concentration of BB dye, C is the concentration of BB after being irradiated for time t, and k is the rate.

The − Ln(C/C0) versus time plots were plotted for all samples, the linearity showed that obeying the pseudo-ﬁrst-order Langmuir–Hinshelwood kinetics. From the experimental work, the photocatalytic efficiency obtained for the ZnO-NFs@GQDs nanohybrid is comparable to results reported in literature where Ebrahimi et al. [46] obtained approximately 2.8 times higher photocatalytic activity using ZnO NWs/GQDs. Fig. 8 (B) shows a graph of the time variation of C/C0 of BB dye (in percentage) degraded in the presence of 1 mL of each of the samples (ZnO-NRs, ZnO-NRs@GQDs nanohybrid, ZnO-NFs and ZnO-NFs@GQDs nanohybrid). The negative values in the graph are as a result of waiting 30 min to achieve equilibrium between adsorption and desporption. The photocatalytic behaviour of the ZnO-NRs was the weakest of all, degrading just 19 % of BB dye in 180 min.. ZnO-NFs@GQDs nanohybrid on the other hand, showed the highest photocatalytic activity of all the samples. As expected the ZnO-NFs@GQDs had the highest photocatalytic activity degrading 80 % of the BB dye in 180 min almost completely decolouring the solution as seen in Fig. 7 (A).

If the synthesized nanohybrids exhibit antimicrobial activity, the nanohybrids have potential of being used as a “dual-nanohybrid” that has photocatalytic and antimicrobial efficiency. The nanohybrids together with the bare ZnO-NFs and ZnO-NRs were tested against four bacterial strains; Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) were used representatives of Gram-negative bacteria, while Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Bacillus cereus (B. cereus) representatives for Gram-positive bacteria. One of the most commonly used methods to quantify bacterial populations is the standard plate count, from which colony-forming unit (CFU) can be calculated. CFU is an estimate of viable bacterial count, which is calculated using Eq. 3. [25].
C F U / m L =
T o t a l
c o l o n i e s
x
d i l u t i o n
f a c t o r
V o l u m e
p l a t e d

It is interesting to note, in antimicrobial applications (Fig. 9 ) the ZnO-NRs out-performs ZnO-NFs with the number of CFU was significantly lower for both ZnO-NRs and the ZnO-NFs@GQDs nanohybrid. The least CFU was calculated for ZnO-NRs@GQDs nanohybrid tested against S. aureus. The general trend of the calculated efficiency was ZnO-NRs@GQDs < ZnO-NRs < ZnO-NFs@GQDs < ZnO-NFs and showed greater sensitivity to the Gram- bacteria compared to Gram-negative bacteria. In terms of the morphology of the nanohybrids, shape plays a big role when it comes to the mechanism of internalization, as such it explains why rods penetrate the surface of the bacteria with much ease compared to NFs. This is also taking into account the ZnO-NFs@GQDs nanohybrid was aggregated after linking GQDs (Fig. 1).

The efficiency of the ZnO-NRs or ZnO-NRs@GQDs nanohybrid can also be explained using active facets, typically spherical NPs have a (100) facets whereas ZnO-NRs are characterized by (111) and (100), it has been demonstrated in literature [47] that NPs with (111) have higher antimicrobial efficiency. The role of GQDs in the nanohybrid was to augment the production of ROS, as such we expected the ZnO-NFs@GQDs nanohybrid to be far efficient than ZnO-NRs without GQDs. However, it was not the case which shows that shape is crucial and as such we propose the inhibition method is initially through internalization (hence the rod-structure is favoured) and this disrupts the cellular function and finally through the production of ROS. The susceptibility of the Gram-positive bacteria to the nanohybrids compared to the Gram-negative is attributed to the structural difference in the cell walls of the bacteria.

We report the successful synthesis of ZnO-NRs, ZnO-NFs and GQDs and the resulting conjugate ZnO-NRs@GQDs and ZnO-NFs@GQDs nanohybrids. Covalently linking GQDs to the nanohybrids reduced the bandgap and also decreased the rate of e−/h+ pairs recombination of the photocatalysts. This was evidenced by a significant decrease in the PL spectra of the nanohybrids. ZnO-NFs@GQDs nanohybrids showed better photocatalytic activity which was attributed to larger surface area which allows more absorption of the solar energy. However, ZnO-NRs had better antimicrobial activity, the inhibition of bacteria was as a result of both ROS and internalization. We postulate shape played a crucial role as increased zones of inhibition were observed in ZnO-NRs compared to the ZnO-NFs and the same was true for ZnO-NRs@GQDs nanohybrids which were found to be better antimicrobial agents. Hence, the inhibition method is initially through internalization (hence the rod-structure is favoured) and this disrupts the cellular function. Finally, we found that the choice of nanohybrid would depend on the ultimate application. It is found that if one is looking for a better photocatalyst, ZnO-NFs@GQDs would be a better candidate and in a similar way if one is looking for excellent biocidal agent, ZnO-NRs@GQDs would be more suitable.

Charmaine Tshangana: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft, Methodology. Mandla Chabalala: Formal analysis. Adolph Muleja: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Edward Nxumalo: Writing - review & editing. Bhekie Mamba: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the partial financial support from University of South Africa and the National Research Foundation of South Africa.
